title: 2D Array Concepts & Grid Traversal
description: Learn how to declare, index, and traverse a 2D array in C using row and column notation.
congratsText: Great work! You now understand how 2D arrays represent grids in memory.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Declare and index a 2D array using [row][col] notation
  • Explain how a 2D array is laid out in memory
  • Traverse a full 2D array using nested loops
  • Print a 2D array in aligned grid form

sections.title: The Classroom Grid

quiz.question: What two numbers are needed to describe exactly one seat in a classroom grid?
options: A row number and a column number
Just a single seat number, counted from 1 to the total
The student's name and their favorite subject
The total number of rows only, with columns assumed

sections.lesson:
The Classroom Grid
To describe exactly one seat in a classroom, you need two coordinates: which row, and which column. A 2D array represents exactly this — a grid addressed by row and column, instead of a single index.

sections.title: Declaring a 2D Array

quiz.question: What does int grid[3][4]; create?
options: A grid with 3 rows and 4 columns, 12 elements total
A grid with 4 rows and 3 columns, 12 elements total
A 1D array of 7 elements combining both numbers
3 separate 4-element arrays that are unrelated to each other

sections.lesson:
Declaring a 2D Array
The first bracket is the number of rows, the second is the number of columns.
int grid[3][4]; // 3 rows, 4 columns, 12 elements total int gradebook[2][3] = { {90, 85, 78}, {88, 92, 75} };

sections.title: Indexing with [row][col]

quiz.question: What does grid[2][3] refer to in int grid[3][4];?
options: The element in row 2, column 3 (both zero-indexed)
The element in row 3, column 2
The total number of elements in row 2
An invalid index, since 2D arrays only support one number

sections.lesson:
Indexing with [row][col]
Just like 1D arrays, 2D arrays are zero-indexed. grid[row][col] reads or writes the element at that specific row and column.
int grid[3][4]; grid[2][3] = 99; // write to row 2, column 3 printf("%d", grid[2][3]); // read it back: 99

sections.title: Row-Major Memory Layout

quiz.question: In row-major order, how is a 2D array actually stored in memory?
options: One entire row after another, laid out as a single continuous block
One entire column after another, laid out as a single continuous block
Each row is stored in a completely separate, disconnected block of memory
Elements are stored in a random order determined at runtime

sections.lesson:
Row-Major Memory Layout
A 2D array isn't really a grid in memory — it's laid out as one long row, with each row of the grid placed right after the previous one. This is called row-major order.
int grid[2][3] = {{1,2,3},{4,5,6}}; // In memory: 1 2 3 4 5 6 — row 0 then row 1, back to back

sections.title: Traversing a 2D Array with Nested Loops

quiz.question: What is the correct order of operations to print every element of grid[3][4] row by row?
options: An outer loop over rows, and an inner loop over columns
A single loop that counts from 0 to 12
An outer loop over columns, and an inner loop over rows
Two separate, unrelated loops that never nest inside each other

sections.lesson:
Traversing a 2D Array with Nested Loops
The outer loop walks through each row; the inner loop walks through each column within that row. Printing a newline after the inner loop finishes is what makes the output look like an actual grid.
for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < 4; c++) { printf("%d ", grid[r][c]); } printf("\n"); }

sections.title: Reading Values into a 2D Array

quiz.question: In this nested input loop, what does scanf fill in first?
options: Every column of row 0, before moving on to row 1
Every row of column 0, before moving on to column 1
The entire grid at once, with a single scanf call
Only the diagonal elements of the grid

sections.lesson:
Reading Values into a 2D Array
Reading follows the same row-by-row order as printing: the inner loop finishes filling every column of the current row before the outer loop advances to the next row.
for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < 3; c++) { scanf("%d", &grid[r][c]); } }

sections.title: Printing an Aligned Grid with Field Widths

quiz.question: Why does printf("%4d", value); help when printing a grid of numbers?
options: It reserves a fixed width per number, keeping columns visually aligned
It rounds the number to 4 decimal places
It limits the value to a maximum of 4 digits, discarding the rest
It has no visible effect on the printed output

sections.lesson:
Printing an Aligned Grid with Field Widths
Without a fixed width, numbers of different lengths (5 vs. 100) make columns drift out of alignment. A field width like %4d pads every number to the same width, keeping the printed grid looking like an actual grid.
for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < 3; c++) { printf("%4d", grid[r][c]); } printf("\n"); }

sections.title: Complete Example: Build and Print a Grade-Book Matrix

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the inner loop's boundary, c < 3, when traversing a 3x3 gradebook?
options: It keeps the inner loop from reading past the last valid column in each row
It controls how many rows the outer loop will process
It has no functional purpose and could be removed safely
It determines the data type stored in the array

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: Build and Print a Grade-Book Matrix
A full 3x3 gradebook matrix, built and printed with the same nested-loop pattern used throughout this discussion.
int gradebook[3][3] = { {90, 85, 78}, {88, 92, 75}, {70, 95, 89} }; for (int r = 0; r < 3; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < 3; c++) { printf("%4d", gradebook[r][c]); } printf("\n"); }

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What is the core relationship between a 2D array and nested loops?
options: An outer loop over rows and an inner loop over columns visits every grid cell exactly once
2D arrays cannot be traversed with loops at all
A single loop is always sufficient to visit every cell
The inner loop must always run more times than the outer loop

sections.lesson:
Recap
A 2D array is addressed with grid[row][col] and stored in row-major order in memory. An outer loop over rows paired with an inner loop over columns visits every cell exactly once — the pattern every matrix operation in the next discussion will build on.
for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) { for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) { printf("%4d", grid[r][c]); } printf("\n"); }
